text: The surgeon inserts three 5mm trocars into the upper abdomen, with the camera positioned through a 12mm trocar at the umbilicus, providing a view of the abdominal entry.

The surgeon grasps the fundus of the gallbladder and retracts it upward toward the head, revealing the liver, gallbladder, stomach, and duodenum. Adhesions of the omentum and mesentery to the liver are dissected. The anterior and posterior peritoneal reflections at the gallbladder infundibulum are opened to expose critical structures, with dissection extending onto the liver bed on both sides.

The surgeon dissects the cystic artery high on the body of the gallbladder, maintaining distance from portal structures to prevent injury to the common bile duct or hepatic artery. The inferior edge of the gallbladder is cleared, isolating the cystic duct and artery. A Maryland dissector is used to dissect behind the cystic artery and cystic duct at the infundibulum junction. The dissection progresses to the cystic plate, achieving the critical view of safety, showing the cystic duct, cystic artery, and cleared gallbladder edge up to the cystic plate.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct while maintaining the critical view of safety. A stone is visible within the cystic duct, consistent with preoperative symptoms. A single clip is placed laterally on the cystic duct, which is then partially opened. The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to extract the stone from the cystic duct and remove it from the abdomen.

Three additional clips are placed medially on the cystic duct, followed by two clips medially and one laterally on the cystic artery. The node of Collot is visible on the artery. The cystic duct and artery are divided, ensuring complete hemostasis. The gallbladder is removed from the liver bed using electrocautery and placed into an endocatch bag.
The surgeon verifies complete hemostasis in the operative field, followed by irrigation of the area to ensure cleanliness and visibility.
